text:
In this work we introduce Graph of Thoughts (GoT) — a framework that allows a large language model (LLM) to scale the number of input questions beyond the linear or tree-like scalability afforded by state-of-the-art frameworks, such as Chain-of-Thought or Tree of Thoughts. Importantly, we show that it is possible to model the reasoning process behind an output, not using a simple linear or tree-like chain of thought, but using an arbitrary graph structure. Here, every "LLM thought" can be viewed as a vertex in the graph, and the edges between them encode the relationships between these LLM thoughts.

While current models are limited to the ToT (Tree-Over-Tree) abstraction, allowing them to only perform a single relation at a time, GoT abstractions allow for more complex reasoning such as: - Combining many individual thoughts into a single, potentially stronger solution - Extracting knowledge across multiple connected ideas - Performing multiple iterations of a feedback loop in order to fully explore a solution space of thoughts GoT outperforms the state-of-the-art model (ToT) on all benchmarking tasks that we tested. Most notably, when given the sorting benchmark, solutions found using the GoT abstraction are 62% better than those found when using the state-of-the-art ToT abstraction; while they are also 31% faster to compute.

A GoT implementation would necessarily be modular and therefore could be extended with other mind schemes for transformations and or prompting. We could thus push the cognition of large language models (LLMs) closer to the genuinely non-linear, network-like model of the human mind and more in line with real brain biological mechanisms at the neuronal level (e.g. recurrence which is essentially complex networking).
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
